paragraphs.text: Brackwater Marsh Conservancy distributes targeted funding to local restoration teams, educators, and emerging coastal scientists working along the Gulf. Three active programs are open for application in the 2024–2025 cycle.

Seed funding for small-scale, community-driven restoration of tidal habitats across the Gulf coast. Awards support living shorelines, marsh replanting, oyster reef pilots, and hydrology repair on parcels under 25 acres.

Supports teachers, school districts, and informal educators developing place-based estuarine curriculum aligned with Gulf coast ecology. Awards fund classroom materials, field trip logistics, and educator stipends.

A one-year fellowship for early-career researchers studying sea-level rise, salinity dynamics, and habitat migration in Gulf tidal systems. Includes a $20,000 stipend plus $5,000 in research expenses and field access.
Since 2019, the Conservancy has distributed more than $2.1M across 47 funded projects. The eleven highlighted below illustrate the geographic and methodological range of our portfolio.

Each program is reviewed by a rotating panel of three scientific advisors, two community partners, and one Conservancy board member. Panels score proposals against published rubrics covering ecological merit, community benefit, feasibility, and long-term stewardship plans.

All scoring rubrics and prior-year award lists are published in our annual report. The Conservancy does not fund overhead costs above 15% of total project budget, and discourages duplicate applications across programs within a single cycle.
A Gulf Coast nonprofit restoring tidal wetlands, advancing estuarine education, and supporting community-led habitat projects since 2009.

2023
          Aransas Bay Living ShorelineCoastal Bend Bays Alliance
          $14,200
          Installed 410 linear feet of oyster-bag breakwater, reducing measured wave energy on marsh edge by 38%

2023
          Cypress Knees Outdoor ClassroomBeaumont ISD
          $5,000
          Built boardwalk classroom serving 240 fifth-grade students annually across three campuses.

2022
          Salt Marsh Scholars ProgramMathis Independent School District
          $5,000
          Developed six-week brackish water curriculum now used by 14 districts statewide.

2022
          Galveston West End Dune RepairFriends of Galveston Island
          $13,800
          Restored 1.2 acres of secondary dune habitat with 4,200 native sea oat plantings.

2022
          Brackish Salinity MappingDr. Priya Venkatesan, Fellowship
          $25,000
          Mapped tidal salinity gradients across Matagorda Bay tributaries; dataset now publicly archived.

2021
          Mangrove Migration StudyDr. Marcus Halloran, Fellowship
          $25,000
          Documented northward expansion of black mangrove into former Spartina zones at four sites.

2021
          Calhoun County Marsh ReplantingLavaca Bay Stewards
          $11,500
          Replanted 2.3 acres of smooth cordgrass with 87% one-year survival rate.

2020
          Junior Stewards Field ProgramCorpus Christi YMCA
          $5,000
          Engaged 180 youth across summer programming with weekly estuary monitoring activities.

2020
          Terrapin Nest Protection PilotSabine Lake Volunteer Network
          $9,400
          Caged 64 diamondback terrapin nests, raising hatch success from 12% to 71% in first season.

2019
          Freshwater Inflow Advocacy ToolkitNueces Estuary Coalition
          $8,200
          Produced public-facing toolkit used in three regional water planning hearings.

2019
          Roseate Spoonbill Roost CensusGulf Birders Cooperative
          $7,600
          Established baseline counts at 11 rookery sites, now repeated annually by trained volunteers.

visibleText: Brackwater Marsh Conservancy Mission Wetlands Volunteer Grants Contact Funding Coastal Stewardship Grant Programs Brackwater Marsh Conservancy distributes targeted funding to local restoration teams, educators, and emerging coastal scientists working along the Gulf. Three active programs are open for application in the 2024–2025 cycle. Active Grant Programs Restoration Tidal Habitat Seed Fund Community-Led Restoration Projects Up to $15,000 Per Project Seed funding for small-scale, community-driven restoration of tidal habitats across the Gulf coast. Awards support living shorelines, marsh replanting, oyster reef pilots, and hydrology repair on parcels under 25 acres. Eligibility Criteria 501(c)(3) organization or fiscally sponsored community group Project site within 50 miles of Gulf coast tidewater Demonstrated landowner or land manager partnership One-year minimum monitoring plan included in proposal Application Opens January 15, 2025 Deadline March 31, 2025 Award Notice May 15, 2025 Term 18 months Past Recipient Aransas Bay Living Shoreline $14,200 awarded in 2023 to install 410 linear feet of oyster-bag breakwater protecting Spartina marsh from boat wake erosion. Education Estuary Education Initiative K–12 Curriculum Development $5,000 Per Award Supports teachers, school districts, and informal educators developing place-based estuarine curriculum aligned with Gulf coast ecology. Awards fund classroom materials, field trip logistics, and educator stipends. Eligibility Criteria Licensed K–12 educator or accredited education nonprofit Curriculum must reach minimum 60 students per cycle Alignment with state science standards required Open-licensing of final materials to the Conservancy library Application Opens August 1, 2024 Deadline October 15, 2024 Award Notice December 1, 2024 Term One school year Past Recipient Salt Marsh Scholars Program $5,000 awarded in 2022 to Mathis ISD to develop a six-week middle school unit on brackish water chemistry and indicator species. Research Coastal Resilience Fellowship Emerging Scientists & Practitioners $25,000 Stipend + Research A one-year fellowship for early-career researchers studying sea-level rise, salinity dynamics, and habitat migration in Gulf tidal systems. Includes a $20,000 stipend plus $5,000 in research expenses and field access. Eligibility Criteria Graduate student or postdoctoral researcher within five years of terminal degree Research focus on Gulf of Mexico coastal resilience Faculty or institutional sponsor required Quarterly site presence at Conservancy field stations Application Opens September 1, 2024 Deadline November 30, 2024 Award Notice February 1, 2025 Term 12 months Past Recipient Dr. Marcus Halloran $25,000 awarded in 2021 for sediment accretion modeling across four black mangrove sites; results published in Estuaries & Coasts. Questions about applying? Our grants team reviews letters of inquiry before formal submission. Reach the program office through the contact page or email grants@brackwatermarsh.org. Past Grant Recipients, 2019–2023 Since 2019, the Conservancy has distributed more than $2.1M across 47 funded projects. The eleven highlighted below illustrate the geographic and methodological range of our portfolio. Year Recipient & Project Award Outcome Summary 2023 Aransas Bay Living Shoreline Coastal Bend Bays Alliance $14,200 Installed 410 linear feet of oyster-bag breakwater, reducing measured wave energy on marsh edge by 38%. 2023 Cypress Knees Outdoor Classroom Beaumont ISD $5,000 Built boardwalk classroom serving 240 fifth-grade students annually across three campuses. 2022 Salt Marsh Scholars Program Mathis Independent School District $5,000 Developed six-week brackish water curriculum now used by 14 districts statewide. 2022 Galveston West End Dune Repair Friends of Galveston Island $13,800 Restored 1.2 acres of secondary dune habitat with 4,200 native sea oat plantings. 2022 Brackish Salinity Mapping Dr. Priya Venkatesan, Fellowship $25,000 Mapped tidal salinity gradients across Matagorda Bay tributaries; dataset now publicly archived. 2021 Mangrove Migration Study Dr. Marcus Halloran, Fellowship $25,000 Documented northward expansion of black mangrove into former Spartina zones at four sites. 2021 Calhoun County Marsh Replanting Lavaca Bay Stewards $11,500 Replanted 2.3 acres of smooth cordgrass with 87% one-year survival rate. 2020 Junior Stewards Field Program Corpus Christi YMCA $5,000 Engaged 180 youth across summer programming with weekly estuary monitoring activities. 2020 Terrapin Nest Protection Pilot Sabine Lake Volunteer Network $9,400 Caged 64 diamondback terrapin nests, raising hatch success from 12% to 71% in first season. 2019 Freshwater Inflow Advocacy Toolkit Nueces Estuary Coalition $8,200 Produced public-facing toolkit used in three regional water planning hearings. 2019 Roseate Spoonbill Roost Census Gulf Birders Cooperative $7,600 Established baseline counts at 11 rookery sites, now repeated annually by trained volunteers. How Funding Decisions Are Made Each program is reviewed by a rotating panel of three scientific advisors, two community partners, and one Conservancy board member. Panels score proposals against published rubrics covering ecological merit, community benefit, feasibility, and long-term stewardship plans. All scoring rubrics and prior-year award lists are published in our annual report. The Conservancy does not fund overhead costs above 15% of total project budget, and discourages duplicate applications across programs within a single cycle.
